mappings.rationaleAI: African history's coverage of Nubia, the Horn, and medieval kingdoms provides context for the Nilotic Sudan's Christian kingdoms and Muslim expansion (A) and Ethiopia's Aksum decline and Solomonid restoration (B).

Ethiopia's coverage of the Kingdom of Aksum, Zagwe and Solomonic dynasties, and resistance to European colonialism directly addresses the decline of Christian Aksum (B.1), the Zagwe dynasty and Solomonid restoration (B.2), and contacts with the Portuguese (B.3).

The Ottoman Empire's expansion into North Africa and the Nile region relates to the spread of Muslim domination and Mamluk attacks (A.2) and the establishment of Turkish garrisons in Ethiopia in the 16th century (B.3).
Portuguese Indian Ocean exploration relates to contacts with the Portuguese (1520-c. 1632) in Ethiopia (B.3).
Egypt's Khedivate and Ottoman incorporation relate to the Egyptian occupation of the Sudan from 1820 (A.4), and Mamluk attacks in the Nilotic Sudan (A.2).
The Byzantine Empire's Orthodox Christianity and eastern Mediterranean connections provide context for the decline of the Christian Aksum empire and its relations with Islamic states (B.1).
The post-classical period's spread of Islam and geographic expansion of civilizations provides context for Muslim expansion in the Nilotic Sudan (A) and Ethiopian dynastic developments (B).
The Islamic Golden Age's expansion of Muslim civilization relates to the spread of Muslim domination in the Nilotic Sudan from c. 639 (A.2).
The Crusades' Mamluk military campaigns relate to Mamluk attacks on the Nilotic Sudan in the 13th and 14th centuries (A.2).
Ancient history's coverage of Nubia and Aksum provides context for the medieval Christian kingdoms that preceded Muslim expansion (A.1).
Ibn Khaldun's theories on nomadic invasions and state formation provide analytical context for Arab nomadic invasion and the spread of Islam in the Sudan (A).
The British Empire's expansion relates to the growth of British influence under Isma'il Pasha during the Egyptian occupation of the Sudan (A.4).
The early modern period's European exploration and contact relates to Portuguese contacts with Ethiopia (1520-c. 1632) (B.3).
The Mongol Empire's destruction of the Abbasid Caliphate and impact on Islamic political structures provides context for the changing dynamics of Muslim domination in the Nilotic Sudan (A.2).
